texts.full_text: Shakespeare’s ineluctable textual modality within the dreamscape of Finnegans Wake. Pelaschiar’s collection does not ignore those inevitable touchstones, but adds to them a sense of the implications of Shakespeare’s plays to a wider range of Joyce’s works than previously recognized, while uncovering theatrical and historical frameworks within which Shakespearean touchstones in Joyce take on new significance. At their most straightforward, some essays appear as fairly traditional influence studies. Giuseppina Restivo argues, for instance, that Joyce’s play Exiles owes a structural and thematic debt to elements of The Tempest. Valérie Bénéjam disentangles the various ways in which Troilus and Cressida underlines the “Cyclops” episode in Ulysses more thoroughly than has been previously understood, analyzing Joyce’s use of spoken dialogue and exploring how political double-meanings emerge rhetorically both in Shakespeare’s history plays and in Joyce’s quasi-Shakespearean recension. texts.full_text: chaos into something human. The face, now nestled within a body composed of fractured celestial maps, seemed to hum with quiet resistance. I added a second skin, this one marbled with gold and ochre, to form a crown of sorts, its edges curling like prayer smoke. Where the feathers met the skin, the colors bled into each other: cobalt dissolving into saffron, vermilion into slate. It wasn’t a person, exactly, but a —a being in the process of stitching itself from shards of cosmos and memory. As I stepped back, the figure’s outstretched hand appeared to grasp not just space, but time itself. A constellation had pooled in the corner of the canvas, accidental yet deliberate, its stars punched from a discarded stencil. I painted one eye fully, leaving the other as a negative space—a void that somehow held more light. The piece felt like a dialogue between collapse and creation. In its chaos, there was intention; in its fragments, a kind of wholeness. I titled it and left it to dry in the dim light of the studio, where dust motes swirled like the first breath of a galaxy. Matthew WilsonLeave a comment Contents 1 What is the main climate in South Korea? 2 What climate zone is South Korea? 3 Is Korea colder than Japan? 4 What season is in Korea? 5 What should I wear in Korea? 6 What is the hottest month in South Korea? 7 Is South Korea really cold? 8 Is South Korea expensive? 9 Does South Korea have 4 seasons? 10 What religion is in South Korea? 11 Is South Korea safe? 12 How cold is South Korea in winter? 13 Where does the BTS live? What is the main climate in South Korea? South Korea has a temperate climate with four distinct seasons. Winters are usually long, cold and dry. Summers are very short, hot, and humid. Typhoons usually passthrough the country during late summer and early autumn, bringing heavy rainfall. The climate can vary slightly depending on location, with the southern parts of the country experiencing milder winters compared to the northern regions. ### What Climate zone is South Korea? South Korea is primarily located in the temperate zone, specifically within the humid continental climate (Köppen climate classification Dwa) in the north and the humid subtropical climate (Köppen climate classification Cfa) in the south. This classification reflects the distinct four seasons and varying temperatures across different regions of the country. ### Is Korea colder than Japan? Generally, South Korea experiences colder winters compared to Japan due to its more northern latitude and inland geography. W

Many kinds of electronic systems make use of magnetic devices including both digital systems, such as memories, and analog systems such as magnetic field sensors. Digital data memories are used extensively in digital systems of many kinds including computers and computer systems components, and digital signal processing systems. Such memories can be advantageously based on the storage of digital symbols as alternative states of magnetization in magnetic materials provided in each memory storage cell, the result being memories which use less electrical power and do not lose information upon removals of such electrical power. Such memory cells, and magnetic field sensors also, can often be advantageously fabricated using ferromagnetic thin-film materials, and are often based on magnetoresistive sensing of magnetic states, or magnetic conditions, therein. texts.full_text: nts who presented with effort-induced thrombosis of the upper limbs. The 1st patient presented with a 2-week history of fever, shortness of breath, and increasing swelling of the neck after strenuous manual work. The 2nd patient presented with a 2-day history of pain, swelling, and discolouration of the right upper limb following a session of intense weight lifting. The 3rd patient presented with a 2-day history of swelling, pain, and discolouration of the left upper limb due to repeatedvigorous use of the arm during a weekend of intensive gardening. Upon examination, all patients exhibited signs of significant congestion in the affected limbs, including tenderness along the course of the veins, increased warmth, and visible dilatation of superficial veins. Diagnosis was confirmed through Doppler ultrasound and computed tomography (CT) scans, which revealed thrombosis in the subclavian or axillary veins. All patients were treated with therapy, primarily using low molecular weight heparin followed by oral warfarin to maintain an international normalized ratio (INR) of 2-3. The first patient required hospitalization due to the presence of systemic symptoms and suspected pulmonary embolism, which was confirmed on CT angiography. This patient received additional treatment with intravenous anticoagulants and close monitoring for complications. The second and third patients were managed as outpatients but had regular follow-ups to monitor their condition and adjust medication doses. All patients were advised to avoid strenuous activities involving the affected limb until resolution of symptoms and normalization of imaging studies. They also received education on the importance of resumption of physical activity and the risks associated with repetitive, high-intensity movements in individuals predisposed to venous thrombosis. Follow-up at 3 months showed significant improvement in all patients, with complete resolution of symptoms and no recurrence of thrombotic events. This case series highlights the importance of recognizing effort-induced upper limb venous thrombosis as a potential complication following intense physical activity, particularly in individuals who may have underlying risk factors s

Thirty-three patients were included (40 implantations). Thirty-one had spinal cord stimulation implantation, two radiofrequency ablation with SCS addition or neuromodulation technique change to dorsal root ganglion and sacral nerve modulation techniques respectively at 12 months follow up time point. Among the first six implanted subjects three showed improved pain intensity on Visual Analog Scale score from baseline by >50% (median reduction of -64%) during their initial followup, however no improvement in function scores were observed; two patients who had complete coverage using a low-frequency SCS did not experience additional benefit and one patient reported increased leg discomfort that led to device deactivation. Among the 27 subjects with ≥18 months follow up period: five showed mean pain intensity reduction >50% from baseline, three experienced <50% relief while seven had either discontinued therapy or were lost for followup at this time point; no improvements in function scores occurred over that timeframe. A low proportion of patients achieved meaningful and durable improvement with high-frequency spinal cord stimulation treatment during 12 months follow up period after implantation as opposed to the expected results observed following traditional SCS therapies (e.g., pain intensity reduction >50% at ~67%). There is no current explanation for these outcomes that may be linked either partially or fully related factors such duration of CRPS diagnosis and time elapsed between symptom onset versus implant date; patient selection criteria including prior treatment history like whether they have had other forms treatments before starting this therapy (e.g., radiofrequency ablation); medications used concomitantly during study participation which can affect outcomes when taking into account drug-related side effects that could potentially confound findings if not accounted for

texts.full_text: Participant pain was assessed with subjective and objective measures before, during, and after the induction of painful stimuli through cold pressor test. The study aimed at exploring whether receiving support from physicians via their expressive behavior can affect how people experience physical discomfort in a controlled experimental setting where actual doctors were not present. Nonverbal supportive behaviors could include maintaining eye contact while nodding affirmatively or leaning forward slightly to indicate engagement and concern, which may convey reassurance despite the absence of verbal communication. Participants rated both intensity (how severe) as well as unpleasantness associated with their pain experience using standard scales at various points throughout this process. The primary outcome was changes in self-reported levels along these dimensions when exposed either high supportive or low/non-supportive nonverbal cues from a physician figure on video, suggesting that even without direct conversation, gestures and body language can have significant therapeutic benefits for patients dealing with acute pain. This research adds to our understanding of the psychological factors influencing physical sensations such as discomfort/pain management strategies beyond conventional medical treatments alone. It

A: The best way is to use the standard CakePHP conventions. If you use anything else, you'll either spend more time explaining your deviation from the norm or you'll face issues when working with other developers who expect certain conventions. In CakePHP, if you have a many-to-many relationship (which seems to be what you're describing), the best practice is to use a `hasMany through` (or join) table. This approach involves creating an additional table that holds foreign keys from both related tables, and it can also hold any extra information specific to the relationship. ### Example Let's say you have `Users` and `Groups`, and each user can belong to multiple groups, and each group can have multiple users. #### Using a Join Table (Recommended) 1. Yet, in truth, 30 minutes of smart reflection pays back richly in performance, durability, and profitability. The CEO Debrief is the tool to help you SLOW down, reduce overwhelm, and ultimately speed up by focusing on the most relevant actions. In fact, research shows an incredible 25% performance acceleration as a result of debrief implementation (Tannenbaum and Cerasoli, 2013) The clarity you get from self-coaching naturally moves you from insight to implementation. Here'e why it works The CEO Debrief is rooted in science around developing mental and emotional skills required to perform at your highest level. When, each week, we are actively looking at what works, our wins, and gratitude we are training ourselves to have an optimistic view on our reality. This comes not from a place of naive optimism, but from the...realistic understanding that by focusing on strengths and successes, we can build resilience and improve overall performance. By regularly practicing self-reflection through The CEO Debrief, leaders develop greater emotional intelligence, which enables them to navigate complex challenges with increased confidence and poise. This intentional focus on what's working well also fosters a growth mindset, allowing high performers to approach obstacles as opportunities for learning and innovation rather than threats to their ego or status quo. texts.full_text: Red Dwarf seemed to be gone forever. However, spurred on by the 'To Be Continued' line at the end of Series 6, Doug Naylor decided to go for a 7th series in 1996. Instead of him alone writing the scripts, however, guest writers featured heavily in this series. However, many had to be turned down. The seventh series was longer than the others, going from 6 episodes to 8. Also, the filming style alters - quite noticeably - and the laugh track was not added until after post-production. Three episodes had a large amount of deleted scenes, and was presented in a special 'Xtended' version, also found on the recently-released DVD. Director Ed Bye, who had left after Series 4, was enticed back nicely. Turns out he never wanted to leave - the only reason he did was because of conflicting schedules. Chris Barrie was a little more difficult. The Brittas Empire, which he stars in, was becoming more popular. In the end, Rimmer returned for four episodes, two of them flashbacks. "Tikka To Ride" explained away how the Dwarfers got back to Red Dwarf after being separated at the end of Series 6 with a time-travel plot involving Lister's ancestor and his TARDIS-like "Chronomonovator." It also introduced Kochanski, played by Sophie Ward. However, her character was not well-received due to lackluster writing. "Ouroboros," written by Paul Alexander (who later wrote for Doctor Who), is often considered one of the strongest episodes in Series VII despite its dark tone and heavy use of flashbacks featuring Rimmer's past life as a woman. It also introduced Kryten 2X4B-5, played by Michael Redfern. "Mummy Dearest," written by Doug Naylor himself after several scripts were rejected due to their poor quality or inappropriate content (including one involving the Dwarfers being turned into cats), is notable for its exploration of Lister's relationship with his mother and her eventual reveal as

texts.full_text: ) ) OPINION v. ) ) VICTOR DAVID RODRIGUEZ, ) Appellant. INTRODUCTION The State of Arizona (“the State”) appeals from the trial court's order granting victor David Rodriguez’s (“Rodriguez”) motion to suppress evidence obtained in an unlawful search and seizure. For the reasons set forth below, we affirm. FACTUAL AND PROCEDURAL BACKGROUND On October 5, 2021, Detective Daniel Lutz of the Gilbert Police Department conducted a warrantless surveillance operation targeting Rodriguez for over two hours from his residence on South Adams Street (“South Adams”). During this time, Lutz observed several vehicles and individuals entering Rodriguez's home without knocking or ringing any doorbell. One such individual was Jessica Smith (“Smith”), who drove her car onto the residential driveway of Rodriguez’s property. After observing that a vehicle had entered South Adams without anyone getting out, Detective Lutz walked around to its front window to investigate and confirm whether someone inside could see him. As he did so, he noticed a small baggie containing an unknown substance in plain view on the passenger seat floorboard (“the contraband”). Lutz then contacted Rodriguez by radioing Officer Robert Gomez of the Gilbert Police Department. Upon receiving information about Detective Lutz's observation, Officer Gomez entered South Adams to investigate. As he approached Rodriguez’s house, a dog ran from it and attacked him twice before being subdued with pepper spray and handcuffs. A second police officer then arrived on scene and assisted in apprehending the aggressive dog. The officers then proceeded into Rodriguez's residence where they discovered additional contraband. I immediately knew what I wanted for baby’s blanket – a simple design of animals starting at A (ant) all through Z. Which is risky… but kind of brilliant. It’s a move that led critics to sort of pan the book, and they are certainly allowed their opinions. Everyone is. My opinion is that, by leaving room around the plots like that, I not only got the story that grabbed me hardest of all—Garth, the artist who swaps bodies so his can rest while his mind continues to work on the collection—but I got three other very interesting plot lines that gave me room to take a break. Spoilers End Here Kevin J. Anderson is a true fixture in the science fiction community. And clearly not just because he shows up at conferences all the time (Seriously, though. All. The. Time. Even in little old middle of nowhere Eastern Washington where I live. There he is.). He’s written over 120 books, including Star Wars EU (Excuse me, Star Wars Legends, thank you so much Disney…), X-Files companions, Dune prequels, and dozens of his own books. Yet somehow, when Hopscotch is brought up… crickets. And hopefully, I can be a little part of the movement to change that. Because, really and truly, the book is brilliant. texts.full_text: Natural Philosophy: Tradition and Innovation in the Academic Milieu, edited by Andrea Sangiacomo, special issue of History of Universities (forthcoming, 2020). Domesticating Descartes, Renovating Scholasticism: Johann Clauberg And The German Reception Of Cartesianism Nabeel Hamid Abstract This article studies the academic context in which Cartesianism was absorbed in Germany in the mid-seventeenth century. It focuses on the role of Johann Clauberg (1622-1665), first rector of the new University of Duisburg, in adjusting scholastic tradition to accommodate Descartes' philosophy, thereby making the latter suitable for teaching in universities. It highlights contextual motivations behind Clauberg's synthesis of Cartesianism with the existing framework such as a pedagogical interest in Descartes as offering a simpler method, and a systematic concern to disentangle philosophy from theological disputes. These motivations are brought into view by situating Clauberg in the closely-linked contexts of Protestant educational reforms in the seventeenth century, and debates around the proper relation between philosophy and theology. In this background, it argues that Clauberg nevertheless retains an Aristotelian conception of ontology for purely philosophical reasons, specifically, t..

texts.full_text: to fill that knowledge gap by studying face-to-face conversations that could evolve into arguments. In just two years, the project produced award-winning researchthat provides new insight into the way people intuitively use speech andbody language totry to resolve conflict. If you hope to defuse conflict, I think its important to first understand what successful communication lookslike, says projectcoordinator Carl Vogel, ofTrinity College Dublin intheRepublic of Ireland. Usingstate-of-the-art equipmentand research techniques,we analysed group dialogue and our findings will be indispensable for technologies trying toecho human communication. Detecting personality types By organising 46trial participants into pairs and asking themto playa version of thegame-show Family Feud, researcherswatched as the groups tried to agree how best to answer a series of questions. Underthe guidance of threefacilitators, the teams conferred with one another as their body languagewas scrutinised to see how speech, facial expression,eye contact and gestures can defuse disagreement. Aswell as learning more about human conversation,the MULTISIMOproject aimed to develop a systemthat could automatically detect personality types. This is important because imagine you are designing a bot to moderatedialogue on socialmedia, for example, says Vogel. You wouldwant that bot to be able to detect whenone useris more dominant so it could adopt strategies to facilitatebetter conversation. To design this system, the project teamfirst hadto learnhow humansperceived eachother.Theyenlisted 13 observers to rate each of the players level of dominance. The first five were asked tomaketheir decisions by watching avideo ofthe entire experiment. The other group were asked to assume the roleof bots,making snapjudgements by just listening to seconds-long snippetsof the footage.
